column3: Ptolemy Philometor, in the 18th year of his reign, granted to Onias, who was the son of Onias the holy High Priest, permission to build a temple at Heliopolis in Egypt, similar to that of Jerusalem, which was called by him the Onion temple; it stood for 233 years, and was finally destroyed together with the Jerusalem temple by Titus and Vespasian. So Josephus, Eusebius, and others.
Philometor, craftily wishing to deprive his son-in-law Alexander of the kingdom of Asia, died suddenly in the year of the Greeks 167 [1 Macc. 11:1 and 18].

Ptolemy Physcon, also called Euergetes, brother of Philometor, reigned after his brother for 29 years. By a strange prodigy he was at once brother, husband, and son-in-law of Cleopatra, for he took her daughter to wife. Moreover, when Cleopatra ruled alone after her husband Physcon had been expelled, Physcon had both his own son and hers cut into pieces, placed in a chest, and served up to her during a banquet on her birthday, as Justin attests in books 38 and 39.
Ptolemy Lathurus, son of Ptolemy Euergetes, surnamed by others Soter, reigned 16 years.
Ptolemy Alexander, brother of Lathurus, reigned 10 years together with his mother, who had expelled Lathurus.
Ptolemy Lathurus, restored to the kingdom on account of his mother's cruelty, reigned again for another 8 years.

Ptolemy called Auletes, because he had practised playing the flute, son of Lathurus according to Salianus. He begot Cleopatra, who was the last queen of Egypt. He reigned together with his daughter Berenice for 30 years in all.
Ptolemy Dionysius, son of Auletes, since he was a minor, ruled Egypt through his sister Cleopatra for 5 years.

Cleopatra, having overthrown her brother Ptolemy Dionysius, reigned alone for 17 years. This is the famous concubine of Mark Antony the triumvir, who fell in battle defeated by Augustus Caesar. Cleopatra, lest she should fall into the hands of Augustus, killed herself by the bite of an asp. Augustus forthwith subjected all Egypt to himself and the Romans, and then became monarch in the year of the city 724, which was the third year of Olympiad 187. All these things are treated by Eusebius, Justin, St. Epiphanius, Bede, Salianus, Torniellus, and others.

These matters will shed great light on both books of Maccabees. For the kings of Asia, Antiochus and Demetrius, long contended with the Ptolemaic kings of Egypt over Judaea and indeed over empire itself; for Judaea lay between the two, and was therefore an apple of discord between them, each striving to claim it for himself.

Chronotaxis of the Maccabees, or Assamonaeans, who ruled the people not only as High Priests but also as Princes: 1. Mattathias, rising against Antiochus Epiphanes in the ninth year of Epiphanes, which was the year of the Greeks 145, held the Pontificate and Principate of Israel for a few months [1 Macc. 2:1].

2. Judas Maccabaeus, after the death of Mattathias, succeeded his father as if firstborn son in the Pontificate and Principate; he defended his country and the true worship of God most bravely and successfully; he purified the Temple after casting out the idol of Antiochus in the year of the Greeks 146; he was finally slain fighting most fiercely against Bacchides in the year of the Greeks 152 [1 Macc. 9:5 and 18]. He ruled and defended Israel for 6 years.

3. Jonathan was appointed successor to his brother Judas by the people, approved afterwards by Alexander Balas, son of Antiochus Epiphanes [1 Macc. 10:5 and 20]. He was finally slain by treachery by Tryphon in the year of the Greeks 170 [1 Macc. 16:23 and 24], having governed for 19 years.

4. Simon was substituted for his brother Jonathan in the Dukedom and Pontificate [1 Macc. 13:8 and 25]. He was slain by treachery by his son-in-law Ptolemy in the year of the Greeks 177 [1 Macc. 16, last verse], having governed for 7 years.

5. John Hyrcanus, son of Simon, presided as duke and High Priest for 31 years. He is mentioned in the last verse of 1 Maccabees, at which point the sacred history of the Maccabees, and indeed of the entire Old Testament and Holy Scripture, comes to an end.

6. Aristobulus, son of John Hyrcanus. He was the first to assume the name and crown of king, which example his later descendants followed. He killed his brother Antigonus, and in that same year died by divine vengeance, vomiting blood. He reigned 1 year.

7. Jannaeus, younger brother of Aristobulus, who, to please the Greeks, assumed a Greek name and was called Alexander. He left two sons, Hyrcanus and Aristobulus, who contended with each other for the kingdom all the way to the time of Herod, and were his instruments of ruin. He reigned 27 years.
8. Alexandra, wife of Jannaeus, on his death usurped the kingdom and reigned for 9 years.

9. Aristobulus the younger, son of Jannaeus, having excluded his elder brother Hyrcanus, seized the Pontificate and kingdom; but he was captured by Pompey and sent to Rome with his two sons Antigonus and Alexander, having reigned for 3 years. Then Pompey took Jerusalem and subjected all Judaea to the Romans, making it tributary, in the consulship of Cicero and Antony.
10. Hyrcanus the elder, son of Jannaeus, was restored to the Pontificate by Pompey, and held it amid various vicissitudes for 22 years.

11. Alexander, son of Aristobulus, having escaped from Rome, attempted to seize Judaea; but after three years was captured by Gabinius and beheaded by Pompey; his father Aristobulus himself also, fleeing from Rome with his other son Antigonus, seized Judaea and the Pontificate with Parthian help for three and a half years. But Julius Caesar adjudicated the Pontificate to Hyrcanus, as Pompey had already done before, and committed to Antipater the guardianship of Idumaea, Judaea, and Hyrcanus, as if making him procurator; then Mark Antony appointed Herod and Phasael, sons of Antipater, as tetrarchs. Finally Antigonus, the last of the Maccabees, was beheaded at Herod's petition after some years.

In Antigonus the entire stock of the Asmonaeans came to an end, as did their kingdom and Pontificate, which they had held for 126 years according to Josephus, or rather for 130 years according to Salianus and others. For so many years elapsed from the ninth year of Antiochus Epiphanes until the first year of Herod, when he, having slain Antigonus, fully occupied the kingdom, and in him the sceptre departed from Judah, and therefore Christ came, according to the oracle of Jacob in Genesis 49:10. All these things are clear from Josephus, Eusebius, Salianus, Torniellus, Pererius, and others. # GEM

Series of High Priests from Alexander the Great to Christ: 1. Jaddus. He met Alexander the Great and reconciled him, who had been hostile to the Jews, as Josephus attests in books 11 and 13 near the end. According to Eusebius he presided for 17 years. Jaddus's younger brother was Manasses, who went to the temple built like that of Jerusalem by his father-in-law Sanballat on Mount Gerizim, and became its High Priest; concerning this there was a perpetual quarrel and dispute between the Jews and the Samaritans all the way to Christ, as is clear from John 4. See Josephus book 11, chapter 7.
2. Onias the first, son of Jaddus, to whom some give 23 years.
3. Simon, son of Onias, surnamed the Just, whose praises are described in Ecclesiasticus 50:1.
4. Eleazar, brother of Simon, who sent 72 interpreters to Ptolemy Philadelphus to translate the Old Testament from Hebrew into Greek.
5. Manasses, son of Onias the first, brother of Simon, and uncle of Eleazar.
6. Onias the second, son of Simon the Just, surnamed the Covetous.
7. Simon the second, son of Onias the second.
8. Onias the third, surnamed the Holy, son of Simon the second, who by his prayer freed Heliodorus when he was being scourged.

And it came to pass, after Alexander (the Great) son of Philip the Macedonian, who FIRST (Greek: πρότερον, that is, previously) REIGNED IN GREECE — namely, over all of it, as Serarius says — "having gone forth from the land of Chittim," that is, from the regions of Judaea beyond the sea (for these the Jews call Chittim), such as Macedonia, Greece, Cyprus, etc. Just as today those who dwell beyond the Alps are called "ultramontanes" by Italians. Again, some take Chittim properly to mean Macedonia (which today is called Albania), both because Philip and the Persians are called kings of the Chittim, that is, of the Macedonians, in 1 Macc. 8:5; and because Suidas, under the words Latini and Κιτιεῖς, asserts that Macedonia is properly so called from Chittim, namely Κίτιον, and in Attic μάκιτον. So also St. Jerome, book 5 on Isaiah ch. 23 and on ch. 27 of Ezekiel and on Daniel ch. 11, takes Chittim to mean the islands of Macedonia; and St. Epiphanius, heresy 30, says Alexander is said to have gone forth from Chittim, that is from Macedonia, because in it was the stock of the Cyprians and Rhodians, to whom this name properly belongs: Chittim.

And he took the spoils of the city, and set it on fire, and destroyed its houses and its walls round about. The city of David, which was the citadel of Jerusalem situated on Mount Zion, was thus plundered, burned, and its fortifications demolished by the forces of Antiochus.

And they led away captive the women, and the children, and the cattle they took possession of. The population was enslaved and the livestock seized as plunder, adding to the devastation already wrought upon Jerusalem.

And they built up the city of David with a great and strong wall and strong towers, and it became a citadel for them. Antiochus transformed the city of David into a fortress, reinforcing it with massive walls and towers so that it might serve as a garrison and stronghold from which to dominate Jerusalem and oppress the Jews.

And they placed there a sinful nation, wicked men, and they were strengthened in it, and they stored up arms and provisions, and gathered together the spoils of Jerusalem. A garrison of impious and lawless men was installed in this citadel, well-armed and supplied, who continually plundered Jerusalem.
And they stored them there; and they became a great snare. The accumulated spoils and the garrison itself became an ongoing trap and source of ruin for the inhabitants of Jerusalem.

And this was done as an ambush against the sanctuary, and an evil adversary in Israel. The citadel thus served as a constant threat and hidden danger menacing the Temple and the entire people of Israel, as a devil or adversary perpetually lying in wait against holy things.

And they shed innocent blood round about the sanctuary, and defiled the holy place. The garrison committed murder around the very precincts of the Temple, polluting by bloodshed the sacred area which God had consecrated to His worship.

And the inhabitants of Jerusalem fled because of them, and the city became the dwelling of strangers, and she became strange to her own seed, and her own children forsook her. So great was the terror inflicted by the garrison that the rightful inhabitants abandoned Jerusalem, which then became populated by foreigners, alien to its own people and forsaken even by those born within her walls.

Her sanctuary was made desolate like a wilderness, her festival days were turned into mourning, her sabbaths into reproach, her honours into nothing. The Temple, once the glory of all nations, lay deserted; the joyful feasts of the Lord became occasions of grief; the sabbath, formerly held sacred, was treated with contempt; and all the dignity of divine worship was reduced to nothing.

According to her glory, so was her dishonour multiplied; and her high estate was turned into mourning. As Jerusalem had formerly been exalted above all cities in honour and sanctity, so now her disgrace was proportionate to her former greatness, and her former majesty was exchanged for lamentation.

And King Antiochus wrote to all his kingdom, that all the people should be one, and that every one should leave his own law. Antiochus, in his pride and impiety, sought to abolish all national religious distinctions and impose a forced religious uniformity throughout his empire, compelling all peoples to abandon their ancestral customs and laws in favour of Greek religion and practice.

And all the nations consented according to the word of King Antiochus. The Gentile peoples, having no firm attachment to divine truth, readily complied with the king's edict, abandoning their various national customs without great resistance.

And many of Israel consented to his service, and they sacrificed to idols, and profaned the sabbath. To their great shame, a considerable number of Israelites apostatised, offering sacrifice to false gods and desecrating the sabbath which God had hallowed as a perpetual sign of His covenant with His people.

And the king sent letters by the hands of messengers to Jerusalem, and to all the cities of Judah, that they should follow the laws of the nations of the earth. Antiochus dispatched official written decrees to Jerusalem and throughout all Judaea, commanding the Jews to conform to pagan Hellenistic customs and to abandon the Mosaic Law entirely.

And that they should forbid holocausts, and sacrifices, and atonements to be made in the temple of God. The perpetual sacrifice ordained by God Himself was to be suppressed; the altar of the Lord was to fall silent, and all the expiatory rites of the Mosaic law were to cease by royal command.

And should forbid the celebration of the sabbath and the solemn days. Even the external observance of the sabbath and the sacred feasts of the Lord — Passover, Pentecost, Tabernacles — were prohibited under penalty of death.

And he commanded the holy places to be profaned, and the holy people of Israel. Not content with suppressing the rites of religion, Antiochus directly commanded the defilement of the Temple itself and the corruption of the people whom God had set apart as His own holy nation.

And he commanded altars to be built, and temples, and idols, and swine's flesh to be sacrificed, and unclean beasts. Pagan altars and idol-temples were to be erected everywhere; swine, the most abominable of animals to the Jews and expressly forbidden by Mosaic law, were to be offered in sacrifice, and other legally unclean animals likewise.

And that they should leave their sons uncircumcised, and let their souls be defiled with all uncleannesses and abominations, to the end that they should forget the law, and change all the ordinances of God. Circumcision, the very sign of the covenant between God and Abraham, was forbidden. The entire fabric of the Mosaic law — dietary regulations, ritual purity, circumcision — was to be destroyed, so that Israel might utterly forget God's covenant and conform entirely to pagan ways.

And whosoever would not do according to the word of King Antiochus should die. The penalty for any observance of the Jewish law was death, making this the most severe and systematic religious persecution the Jewish people had yet endured.

According to all these words he wrote to all his kingdom, and he appointed overseers over the people who should force them to do these things. Royal commissioners were appointed throughout the realm with authority to compel compliance with the edict by force, ensuring that no Jewish community could quietly maintain its ancestral observances.

And they commanded the cities of Judah to sacrifice. The local authorities throughout Judaea received direct orders to compel the Jewish populace to offer pagan sacrifices, thus publicly apostasising from the God of their fathers.

And many of the people were gathered to them, who had forsaken the law of the Lord, and they committed evils in the land. Apostates from among the Jews themselves joined the persecutors, and these renegades proved especially cruel oppressors of their faithful brethren, as those who abandon true religion often become the most bitter enemies of those who remain faithful.

And they drove away the people of Israel into lurking places, and into the hidden places of fugitives. The faithful remnant of Israel was hunted down and driven into caves, deserts, and other hiding places — reduced to the condition of outlaws and fugitives in their own land.

On the fifteenth day of the month Casleu, in the hundred and forty-fifth year, King Antiochus set up the abominable idol of desolation upon the altar of God, and throughout all the cities of Judah round about they built altars. On the 15th of Casleu (corresponding roughly to our December) in the Seleucid year 145 (i.e., 168/167 BC), Antiochus erected upon the very altar of burnt offering in the Temple the idol called "the abomination of desolation" — fulfilling the prophecy of Daniel (Dan. 9:27; 11:31; 12:11) — and pagan altars were constructed in every city of Judaea.

And before the doors of the houses, and in the streets, they burnt incense and sacrificed. Pagan worship was carried into the most public and domestic spaces: incense was burned and sacrifices offered in the streets and before private houses, so that the entire land was publicly given over to idolatry.

And they burnt the books of the law of God with fire, tearing them in pieces. The sacred scrolls of the Torah — the word of God entrusted to Israel — were systematically sought out, torn apart, and burned, in a calculated attempt to eradicate the very memory of divine revelation from the people.

And whosoever was found with the books of the testament of the Lord, and whosoever observed the law of the Lord, according to the edict of the king, was put to death. Possession of the scriptures and observance of the law were capital offences. This was among the most extreme acts of religious tyranny recorded in ancient history, foreshadowing later persecutions of Christians.

With power they did these things against the people of Israel who were found in every city month after month. The enforcement was systematic and continuous — carried out with military force throughout every city, month after month, allowing no respite to the faithful.

And on the five and twentieth day of the month they sacrificed upon the altar which was over against the altar of God. On the 25th of each month — the birthday of Antiochus — a solemn pagan sacrifice was offered upon the idol altar erected over against the true altar of God, as a deliberate and calculated affront to the worship of the God of Israel.

And the women that circumcised their children were slain according to the commandment of King Antiochus. Mothers who had their sons circumcised in obedience to God's covenant with Abraham were executed — a barbarous cruelty that reveals how fiercely Antiochus targeted the most fundamental expressions of Jewish identity and covenant fidelity.

And they hanged the children by the neck in all their houses, and those who had circumcised them they killed. The infants themselves were hanged from their mothers' necks after the mothers were executed, and all who had performed the circumcision were likewise slain — a scene of horror that moved all Israel to lamentation.

And many of the people of Israel determined with themselves that they would not eat unclean things, and chose rather to die than to be defiled with unclean meats. # GEM Here we see the first clear historical instances of what later theology would call martyrdom for religion: men choosing death rather than transgress the law of God. The Fathers, particularly St. Ambrose and St. Augustine, cite these martyrs of the Maccabean period as true martyrs, dying for the divine law, and as forerunners of the Christian martyrs.

And they would not break the holy law of God, and were slain. Their death was not stubbornness or fanaticism, but genuine martyrdom — the willing acceptance of death rather than the violation of God's holy commandments. They are honoured by the Church among the saints.

And there was very great wrath upon the people. This wrath (ira) is understood by commentators in two senses: first, the wrath of Antiochus against those who resisted; second, and more deeply, the permissive wrath of God, who allowed this tribulation to fall upon His people as a chastisement for their sins and an occasion for purification, as He had done in the time of the Babylonian captivity.

Kittim in v.1 refers to the land from which Alexander came. Lapide discusses the identification of "Kittim": Cyprus is one candidate, as are other overseas regions which the Hebrews called "islands" (insulae), including Italy, Greece, and Macedonia. He notes Livy (Decade 2, book 5) mentions a place called Citium in Macedonia where a whole army gathered. He also notes linguistic affinity between "Macedonia" and "Cethim": Dionysius and Hesychius in his Lexicon call Macedonia "Macela" and the Macedonians "Macetas." Ausonius (Epigr. 2) writes "Macedonianque attollere nomen." Thucydides (2.2) says Cyprians migrated to Macedonia and could have given it the name Cethim. Theodore Annius, Mantus, and Fererius on Isaiah 2 all take Cethim to mean Macedonia.

Darius king of the Persians — the last one, surnamed Codomannus. Alexander the Great struck him down and killed him in the sixth year of his reign, thereby transferring the monarchy from Darius and the Persians to himself and the Greeks; having become sole monarch he reigned another six years, dying in his twelfth regnal year. Darius was previously called Codamus and kept a tavern at Susa (Dio Chrysostom, Oration 5); thence he was made prefect of Armenia and finally king. He was therefore not of royal lineage but pretended to be by assuming the royal name of Darius — for which reason Alexander invaded and deprived him of a throne not rightfully his.
And he passed through to the ends of the earth — subjugating everything by arms all the way to the Ganges and the Indian Ocean, according to Josephus, Livy, and Justin.

And the earth was silent before him — out of terror at his strength and his unbroken victories, so that no one dared resist him or open his mouth against him, just as mice are silent before a cat and dogs before a lion.

And he gathered forces and an army — that is, an army formidable not so much by numbers as by the valour of its soldiers. For Alexander's troops were veterans, hardened by labours and battles, emboldened by continuous victories, so that they swept through and subdued everything. This is a hendiadys; hence the Greek reads: "he gathered valour," meaning a brave army.

And his heart was lifted up — to such a degree that he aspired to divinity and claimed he had been begotten by Jupiter himself. He had absorbed this vain opinion from his immense victories, the flattery of courtiers, and success beyond all belief, so that — as Angellius records (book 13, ch. 4) — he made his mother Olympias the concubine of Juno. In his folly he wrote to the Greeks commanding them to declare him a god, as the sources attest. # GEM

Some believed, he says, that the provinces were distributed by the testament of Alexander; and he asserts that this was handed down by various authors, though he himself holds the contrary opinion. But to our Author, to whom the Greek and Latin Fathers, indeed the whole Church, attribute sacred authority as though he were a hagiographic writer, greater credence must be given.

They built a gymnasium in Jerusalem according to the laws of the nations, in which young men were taught not only the fables and literature of the Gentiles, as SS. Thomas and Lyran hold, and likewise to fence, shoot arrows, compete in running, jumping, discus, and wrestle in the palaestra, etc., but also were instructed in every form of licentiousness, even the unnatural practices of pederasty and sodomy; whence to these gymnasia were added ephebeia or brothels, as in 2 Macc. 4:12. And such places were erected by impious kings of old in Judah, which are called the houses of the effeminate, over which the mother of Asa king of Judah presided as a kind of priestess and princess (3 Kings 15:12); but Asa and other pious kings abolished them, restoring [purity].

The companion of true religion is purity and chastity; so the companion of false religion and heresy is impurity and lust: certainly you will find troops of virgins, both men and women, who have vowed chastity to God and religiously observe it throughout their whole life, nowhere but in the orthodox faith.

AND THEY MADE THEMSELVES FORESKINS,] that is, they drew back by certain instruments and medicines the foreskins cut off in circumcision, and caused them to grow again, so that by this act they might avoid circumcision and Judaism, and profess Gentilism; for the restoration of the foreskin was the symbol of this thing. See what is said on 1 Corinthians 7:18, on the words: Let him not draw over the foreskin.

AND THE KINGDOM OF ASIA AND SYRIA WAS PREPARED IN THE SIGHT OF ANTIOCHUS (Epiphanes); for Seleucus Philopator, the elder brother of Epiphanes, was slain by the treachery of Heliodorus; wherefore Epiphanes, aided by the neighboring kings Eumenes and Attalus, easily seized alone the kingdom of his father. Not content with this: HE BEGAN TO REIGN IN THE LAND OF EGYPT,] for as follows:

AND HE ENTERED INTO EGYPT WITH A GREAT MULTITUDE,] that is, a copious one, namely with a numerous and great army. There is an enallage here; for continuous quantity is put for discrete, namely gravis, or great, for many. A most grievous swarm of flies is called very numerous, Exodus 8:24. See what is said there. Moreover, Antiochus Epiphanes entered Egypt twice, pretending he wished to aid Ptolemy Philometor, his nephew by his sister Cleopatra, who was a child, but in reality with the intention of excluding his nephew and adding the kingdom of Egypt to his own kingdom of Asia. He entered the first time in the second year of his reign, of which 2 Maccabees 4:21 treats. The second time, in the sixth year of his reign, which was the year 142 of the Greeks, of which this passage treats; for in the following year, 143, he went up from Egypt into Israel, as is said in verse 21.

AND HE ENTERED INTO THE SANCTUARY,] that is, the sanctuary; namely the Temple most holy to the Jews, and from it he sacrilegiously removed THE GOLDEN ALTAR, that is the altar of incense; and THE GOLDEN TABLE, upon which the 12 Tribes offered to God the 12 LOAVES OF PROPOSITION, AND THE LIBATION VESSELS, that is the cups, mixing bowls, and other vessels by which wine, oil, and the blood of victims were poured out to God, that is, poured at the horn of the altar.

AND HE MADE A SLAUGHTER OF MEN, namely, as is said in 2 Maccabees 5:12: There were slaughters of young men and old men and women, and the destruction of children and the killing of virgins and infants. Now in the space of three whole days, 80,000 were slain, thousands were bound, and no fewer were sold. The cause was that the Romans had ordered Antiochus Epiphanes to withdraw from Egypt and leave it free to Ptolemy Philometor, the king and their ally; for Publius Popilius Laenas, a Roman legate who had previously been a friend of Antiochus Epiphanes at Rome, having arrived by ship at Alexandria, drew a circle around Antiochus in the sand with a rod and said to him: The Senate and people of Rome command that you withdraw from Egypt and not make war upon the Ptolemies; and that in this very spot you answer what course you intend: whether you desire peace or war. Terrified by this, he replied: If this is the pleasure of the Romans, I must withdraw. And so he immediately moved his army. So Josephus, book 12 of the Antiquities.
[On Antiochus gnashing his teeth and moving camp against the neighboring Jews, pouring out his indignation and fury upon them]
[On "he spoke with great pride": boasting as though he were some earthly god whom all must obey and none could resist, as his antitype Antichrist will do. See Daniel 11:36]

[On "the beauty of women was changed": both because they laid aside their fine garments and put on mourning dress, black and squalid, in common lamentation; and because fear, grief and anguish made them pale and wan. So Jeremiah in Lamentations 1 says of the virgins: "all her beauty is departed from the daughter of Sion"; and of the young men ch.4 v.2: "The sons of Sion, glorious and clothed in fine gold, how are they esteemed as earthen vessels"; and v.7 of the Nazarites: "Her Nazarites were whiter than snow, purer than milk, ruddier than ancient ivory, more polished than sapphire; their face is blacker than coals and they are not known in the streets"]
[On "the land was moved": Judaea was struck and shaken with great terror, grief and anguish, on account of the Jewish inhabitants slain, captured and sold by Antiochus]

[On "after two years of days": after two full years with all their days completely elapsed, so that even some additional days ran over; Antiochus Epiphanes sent the chief collector of tribute, namely Apollonius, to the Jews not merely to plunder but to slaughter them, as is said in 2 Maccabees 5:26]
[On Apollonius setting Jerusalem, already plundered by himself, on fire, and pulling down its houses and walls all around, lest it be able to rebel against Antiochus]
[On "they built the city of David": that is, the citadel of Zion, which dominated the whole city lying below it like a cliff or tower, with a great, high and strong wall]
[On "they placed there a sinful nation": that is, rapacious, murderous and wicked soldiers, who would plunder, bind and slaughter the Jews]

[On "this became an ambush against the sanctuary": that is, against the temple, so that they might lie in wait for Jews going to the temple for sacrifice or prayer, and rushing out from the citadel would attack, plunder, capture and wound them; for Antiochus wished to abolish the worship of God and compel the Jews to adore his idols, and had therefore placed the image of Jupiter Olympius in the temple]
[On "and a wicked devil against Israel": both because these soldiers were devils, that is, calumniators and accusers]

For they were falsely accused when going to the temple, as though they wished to fortify it against Antiochus; and because the soldiers themselves, like madmen, plundered, beat, and tore them apart, so that they filled and polluted the very temple with the blood of the slain; and finally because they compelled them away from the worship of God to the worship of Jupiter, that is, of the devil, and at the same time invited them to fornication in brothels adjoined to the temple; which was a work plainly diabolical.

And King Antiochus inscribed a decree, by which he commanded that all the people should be one, that is, uniform in faith and religion, so that all his subjects should be united with him in the same superstition and idolatry, just as they were united in the same kingdom; and therefore that every man should leave his own law, namely that the Jews should abandon the law and worship of their God given through Moses, and embrace the law and worship of Antiochus. It is expedient that in one kingdom there be one religion, but a true and orthodox one. For if it be false, it is expedient that there be many, so that the true one may shine forth and be recognized from the variety of the false ones, as Cardinal Hosius used to say.

And many of Israel consented to his service. In Greek τῇ λατρείᾳ, that is, to his latria, or sacred service, worship and adoration, namely that they should worship no other God with latria, nor serve any other God, than the God of Antiochus their king, namely Jupiter Olympius, and Antiochus himself. For he wished to be worshipped together with Jupiter as a God.
And the king sent books, that is, letters and epistles. For this is what the Hebrew sepher, meaning book, signifies.

And he sent decrees, by which he commanded that the holy things should be defiled, that is, that the holy temple should be violated and profaned, along with its holy sacrifices, rites, sabbaths, feasts, etc., and the holy people of Israel, so that, having cast aside the holy faith and law of God, they might defile themselves with the sacrilegious victims and superstitions of the gentiles and of idols; whence, explaining this, he adds:

That swine's flesh should be sacrificed, that is, sows and pigs which were unclean by the law of Moses and therefore forbidden to be eaten and sacrificed; and common cattle, that is, unclean ones; for those that were unclean were common to the gentiles, while those that were clean were peculiar and proper to the Jews, Leviticus 11. Hence God, wishing to convert St. Peter to bring Cornelius and the unclean gentiles to Christ, commanded him in a vision to eat unclean animals, saying: What God hath cleansed, call not thou common, that is, unclean, Acts 10:15.

And that they should change all the justifications of God, that is, that they should violate all the ordinances and laws of God; for these are called justices and justifications, because they prescribe what is equitable and just.

And many were gathered together to those who had forsaken the law of the Lord, joining with the apostates in apostatizing from the law of God, namely Jason, Alcimus, Menelaus, and the other leaders of apostasy.

And they drove out of the people of Israel those who refused to apostatize, but steadfastly persevered in the true law and worship of God, so that they were compelled to flee to dens and caves, and to hide themselves there.

On the fifteenth day of the month Casleu (which corresponds to our November), he built and set up upon the altar of God in the temple of Jerusalem the abominable idol of desolation, namely of Jupiter Olympius, which was to be the cause of all calamity and desolation, as Daniel had foretold, ch. 11:11.

By his own power (in Greek ἐν τάχει, that is by his own swift force, that is, violently), the satellites of Antiochus acted against the people of Israel, who were found in every city month by month; by month understand the new moon, or the first day of the month, which was solemn and as it were sacred to the Jews; wherefore they then flowed together from villages and farms into the city to hear the law of God, just as the inhabitants of the countryside near Rome flowed together to Rome on the same day, whence the Calends took their name, as I showed at Leviticus 28:11. The meaning is, as if to say: the soldiers of Antiochus watched for those who observed the new moon according to the law of Moses, and severely punished them, indeed slaughtered them. And in order to abolish the new moon, that is, the solemnity of the first day of the month,

On the twenty-fifth day of the month they sacrificed upon the altar erected to Jupiter Olympius, which was placed opposite the altar of holocausts dedicated to God in the temple of Jerusalem. Upon this altar therefore they sacrificed not only to Jupiter but also to Antiochus himself as if to a God. For he wished to be worshipped as a God, as Daniel foretold ch. 11:36; and therefore he was a type of Antichrist. The idolaters were unwilling to sacrifice upon the altar of God, lest they should seem to sacrifice to the God of the Hebrews; and in order to make it appear as it were profane to the people.

They chose rather to die and to be variously tortured than to be defiled with unclean foods, that is, than to eat swine's flesh and similar meats forbidden in Leviticus 11. For this reason Eleazar and the seven Maccabee brothers were tortured, as recounted in 2 Maccabees 7. All these therefore were glorious in that ancient and primitive time.
Note that the abomination of desolation placed in the Temple was the statue of Jupiter Olympius, before which, on a nearby altar erected to Jupiter by the same persons, they offered sacrifice.

# GEM The early martyrs chose torment and death over the violation of the dietary laws, showing that external bodily purity, as prescribed in Leviticus, was a figure and safeguard of interior purity of soul, and that fidelity to the whole Law, even in its ceremonial precepts, was worth dying for when compulsion to transgress them was tantamount to apostasy.

Daily they were killed with most cruel tortures and torments, lashed also with whips and their bodies lacerated, and while still alive and breathing they were fastened to crosses; moreover their wives and their circumcised children were strangled according to the royal command, and were hung from the necks of their crucified parents.

And from the learned shall fall, that they may be tried, and chosen, and made white until the appointed time. (Dan. 11:35) — i.e., God, indignant at the Jews on account of their crimes, permitted them to be so atrociously afflicted by Antiochus.
In those days arose Matthias the son of John, the son of Simon, a priest of the sons of Joarib, from Jerusalem, and he withdrew to the mountain of Modin.
And he had five sons: John, who was surnamed Gaddis;
and Simon, surnamed Thassi; [the text continues listing the sons of Matthias]
And Matthias said: Woe is me! wherefore was I born to see the ruin of my people, and the ruin of the holy city, and to sit there when it is given into the hands of enemies?
The holy places are fallen into the hands of strangers: her temple is become as a man without honour.
The vessels of her glory are carried away captive: her old men are slain in the streets, and her young men are fallen by the sword of the enemies.
What nation hath not inherited her kingdom, and gotten her spoils?
All her ornaments are taken away. She that was free is become a slave.
And behold our sanctuary, and our beauty, and our glory is laid waste, and the Gentiles have defiled them.
To what end therefore should we live any longer?
And Matthias and his sons rent their garments, and they covered themselves with haircloth, and made great lamentation.
Then they that were sent from king Antiochus came thither, to compel them that were fled into the city of Modin, to sacrifice, and to burn incense, and to depart from the law of God.
And many of the people of Israel consented, and came to them: but Matthias and his sons stood firm.
And they that were sent from Antiochus answering, said to Matthias: Thou art a ruler, and an honourable and great man in this city, and adorned with sons and brethren.

Come thou therefore first, and obey the king's commandment, as all nations have done, and the men of Juda, and they that remain in Jerusalem: and thou, and thy sons, shall be in the number of the king's friends, and enriched with gold and silver, and many presents.
And Matthias answered, and said with a loud voice: Although all nations obey king Antiochus, so as to depart every man from the service of the law of his fathers, and consent to his commandments:
I and my sons, and my brethren will obey the law of our fathers.
God be merciful unto us: it is not profitable for us to forsake the law, and the justices of God.
We will not hearken to the words of king Antiochus, neither will we sacrifice, and transgress the commandments of our law, to go another way.
Now as he left off speaking these words, there came a certain Jew in the sight of all to sacrifice to the idols upon the altar in the city of Modin, according to the king's commandment.
And Matthias saw and was grieved, and his reins trembled, and his wrath was kindled according to the judgment of the law, and running upon him he slew him upon the altar:
Moreover the man whom king Antiochus had sent, who compelled them to sacrifice, he slew at the same time, and pulled down the altar.
And he showed zeal for the law, as Phinees did to Zamri the son of Salomi. # GEM
And Matthias cried out in the city with a loud voice, saying: Every one that hath zeal for the law, and maintaineth the testament, let him follow me.
So he, and his sons fled into the mountains, and left all that they had in the city.
Then many that sought after judgment and justice went down into the desert:
And they abode there, they and their children, and their wives, and their cattle; because afflictions increased upon them.

And it was told to the king's men, and to the army that was in Jerusalem in the city of David, that certain men who had broken the king's commandment were gone away into the hidden places in the wilderness, and that many were gone after them.
And they went out to them straightway, and set the battle in array against them on the sabbath day.
And they said to them: Do you still resist? come forth, and do according to the edict of king Antiochus, and you shall live.
And they said: We will not come forth, neither will we obey the king's edict, to profane the sabbath day.
And they made haste to give them battle.
But they answered them not, neither did they cast a stone at them, nor stopped up the secret places,
Saying: Let us all die in our innocency, and heaven and earth shall be witnesses for us, that you put us to death wrongfully.
So they gave them battle on the sabbath: and they were slain with their wives and their children, and their cattle, to the number of a thousand persons.
And Matthias and his friends heard of it, and they mourned for them exceedingly.
And every man said to his neighbour: If we all do as our brethren have done, and fight not for our lives and our laws against the heathens: they will now quickly root us out of the earth.

And they determined in that day, saying: Whosoever shall come up against us to fight on the sabbath day, we will fight against him: and we will not all die, as our brethren that were slain in the secret places.
Then was assembled to them the congregation of the Assideans, the stout men of Israel, every one that had a good will for the law.
And all they that fled from the evils were added to them, and were a support to them.
And they gathered an army, and slew the sinners in their wrath, and the wicked men in their indignation: and the rest fled to the nations for safety.
And Matthias and his friends went round about, and threw down the altars:
And they circumcised all the children whom they found in the confines of Israel that were uncircumcised: and they did valiantly.
And they pursued after the children of pride, and the work prospered in their hands:
And they recovered the law out of the hands of the nations, and out of the hands of the kings: and they yielded not the horn to the sinner.
Now the days of Matthias drew near that he should die, and he said to his sons: Now hath pride and chastisement gotten strength, and the time of destruction, and the wrath of indignation:
Now therefore, O my sons, be ye zealous for the law, and give your lives for the covenant of your fathers.
And call to remembrance the works of the fathers, which they have done in their generations: and you shall receive great glory, and an everlasting name.
Was not Abraham found faithful in temptation, and it was reputed to him unto justice?
Joseph in the time of his distress kept the commandment, and he was made lord of Egypt.
Phinees our father, by being fervent in the zeal of God, received the covenant of an everlasting priesthood. # GEM
Jesus, whilst he fulfilled the word, was made ruler in Israel.
Caleb, for bearing witness before the congregation, received an inheritance.
David by his mercy obtained the throne of an everlasting kingdom.
Elias, whilst he is zealous for the zeal of the law, was taken up into heaven. # GEM
Ananias [and his companions kept the commandment, and were delivered from the flame]

In those days Mattathias went out — the name Mattathias, or by crasis Matthias as Josephus and Theodoret call him, is Hebrew meaning "gift of God," and is therefore equivalent to the Greek Theodosius, Theodorus, or inversely Dorotheus, and in Latin Adeodatus; for this man was given by God as a great gift, to be a leader of Israel in resisting Antiochus.

Question: Who and what sort of man was Mattathias? Answer: He was a priest of the first class from the tribe of Levi, specifically from the line of Joarib, which is placed first among the priestly families in 1 Par. 24:7. Moreover, this Mattathias, chosen and impelled by God and approved by the people, became High Priest and leader and avenger of the people against their common enemy Antiochus; for after the return from captivity by command of Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes, the same persons were both Pontiffs and leaders of the people, as Josephus testifies in book 11.

You therefore, sons, be strengthened and act manfully in the law; for in it you shall be glorious. — This is the dying exhortation of Mattathias to his sons, urging them to courage and fidelity to the law.

And behold Simon your brother, I know that he is a man of counsel; hear him always, and he shall be a father to you. — Mattathias commends Simon as a man of wise counsel, worthy to serve as a father-figure and guide to his brothers.

And Judas Maccabaeus, strong in might from his youth, shall be your prince of war, and he shall conduct the battle of the people. — Judas is designated military commander on account of his lifelong valor.
And you shall gather to you all those who observe the law, and avenge the wrong done to your people. — They are to rally the faithful and exact justice on behalf of Israel.
Render recompense to the Gentiles, and attend to the commandment of the law. — The duty of holy warfare against the nations is linked inseparably to fidelity to the divine law.
And he blessed them, and was gathered to his fathers. — Mattathias died with a patriarchal blessing, in the manner of the ancient fathers.

And he died in the year one hundred and forty-six, and was buried by his sons in the sepulchres of his fathers at Modin, and all Israel mourned for him with great mourning. — The year 146 of the Seleucid era marks his death; his burial at Modin, the ancestral town, and the universal lamentation of Israel attest to his greatness.

*2G, and 2 Esdras 9:36. For even if Mattathias was not descended from the nearest stock of the Pontiffs, nevertheless, when that line failed, the Pontificate devolved upon him as a priest of the first rank. For Onias the Third, surnamed the Holy, had already been killed in the fifth year of Antiochus, which was the 146th year of the Greeks. Onias's son, namely the younger Onias, had fled into Egypt and had been made Pontiff at Heliopolis of a new temple, which was called Onion after him. But Jason, the brother of Onias, having become an apostate, was compelling the rest to apostatize and cross over to Gentilism. Wherefore it fell to Mattathias to take up the Pontificate and the Leadership of the people, in order to defend and champion the religion as well as the commonwealth brought into extreme danger by Antiochus. Especially since there was no other who dared to do so, but all were either hiding and lying low out of fear of Antiochus, or fleeing away. Therefore God Himself roused him and added courage to him for undertaking this, that he might preserve the faith and worship of God and the safety of the fatherland. He did this in the ninth year of Antiochus, which was the 145th year of the Greeks. Wherefore the Chaldean paraphrase on Canticles 6:6, Josephus, Josippus, Aben-Ezra, and the Hebrews call Mattathias the High Priest or Pontiff, and this both from the designation of God, from the election of the people, and from the right of succession and kinship.

Lapide notes that the sons of Mattathias helped their father in this cause; for the soldiers of Apollonius were slain along with him. Mattathias rightly did this, at God's instigation, acting as judge and avenger of Israel and of religion and the worship of God; and by this deed he entered upon and consecrated his pontificate and leadership, even as Elijah made himself champion of the faith by slaying the priests of Baal (3 Reg. 18), and as Moses with the Levites by slaying the worshippers of the golden calf (Exod. 32), to whom he accordingly said: "You have consecrated your hands today to the Lord, each man in his son and in his brother, that a blessing may be given to you." And as Phinehas by slaying the prince sacrificing to Priapus (Num. 25). See what is said there.

# GEM Thus concerning a blasphemer St. Chrysostom judges and says: "If you hear anyone blaspheming God, approach, rebuke him; and if it is necessary to inflict blows, do not refuse; strike his face with a blow, crush his mouth; sacrifice your hand with the blow." Indeed zeal purges crimes; nay, it sanctifies and consecrates that act, and makes it as it were a holocaust, which is offered to the justice and divine vengeance as a sweet savour.

Remarkable was the courage, fortitude, and audacity of Mattathias in this slaughter, by which he alone, almost single-handedly, opposed himself to a powerful, proud, and cruel king, and impelled the others to oppose themselves, generously exposing his fortunes, family, children, reputation, and life for God, for the faith, for the fatherland. He was therefore a renowned hero, and made his five sons heroes, when he led them forth into battle against the tyrant; but faith and hope in God animated him and made him superior to all dangers, so that like a lion with his five cubs he crushed all his enemies.

And he cried out: Whosoever hath zeal for the law, making good the covenant — striving that the covenant, that is, the bond established with God, should stand in its full force. As if to say: Whoever wishes religion and the commonwealth to be saved, let him go forth after me, let him follow me as leader. Here Mattathias sounds the trumpet-call and summons all to holy war, that in it they may either conquer bravely or fall nobly for God, faith, and fatherland. Thus at Rome C. Marius, opposing Sulla who was attacking the commonwealth, cried out: "Whoever wishes the commonwealth to be saved, let him follow me" (which words we still read inscribed in large letters on the stone lintel of the Porta Angelica at Rome, by which he himself went forth). Having spoken, Marius went out to a neighbouring hill and gathered forces. The same cry was raised by Scipio after the disaster received at Cannae, and by Nasica from the Capitol against Gracchus, as Livy, Paterculus, and Valerius Maximus attest.

And he fled himself and his sons — out of Jerusalem, where he had dwelt as a priest and had struck down the Jew sacrificing to idols at the altar; he fled into the mountains, living among wild beasts and feeding on wild plants as food, lest they should be partakers of defilement, as is said in the book [following]. Regarding the formula "Whoever wishes the republic to be safe, let him follow me," Servius notes on Aeneid book 8 that if there were a tumult — that is, much fear arising from proximity of danger — since there was no time to interrogate individual soldiers, the general who was to lead the army to the Capitol would bring forth two standards, one red, which summoned the infantry, and another blue, which was the sign of the cavalry, and would say: "Whoever wishes the republic to be safe, let him follow me." So likewise Mattathias, calling all zealous for the Law to follow him, cast himself before danger, as Agathias attests in book 2, [writing of one] throwing himself before the emperor against the Gauls.

Then many descended seeking judgment and justice — that is, seeking from Mattathias what was equitable and just: what, namely, one ought to do in such a pressing moment of persecution — whether to endure exile, hunger, cold, nakedness, wounds, and every hardship, even death itself, rather than yield to the tyrant and apostatize from God, His law, and His worship to idols.

Let us all die in our innocence — that is, in the simple observance of the Sabbath, so that on that day we might keep the full rest prescribed by God, to such a degree that on it we would not fight, nor even defend ourselves against those attacking us, but allow ourselves to be slain like simple and innocent lambs. They said this not out of superstition, nor out of imprudence, as the Novatians would have it, but out of zeal for perfect religion. For the Sabbath was held in the highest religious regard among the Jews. Wherefore, although they could by right have fought and defended themselves on that day, yet out of zeal for religion they preferred to offer themselves to death rather than violate the worship and rest of the Sabbath even by the mere appearance and shadow of such violation — because by this means they became martyrs; and martyrdom is the noblest act, and therefore far nobler than fighting and mere survival. The same was done by Jews attacked by Ptolemy Lagus, and afterward by Mithridates, as Josephus recounts in Book 12. # GEM

St. Ambrose praises this in De Officiis, bk. 1, ch. 40. Thus the Theban Legion, under the command of St. Maurice, could have resisted the Emperor Maximian; but they refused, and throwing down their arms permitted themselves to be slain like sheep, because the legionary faithful preferred to be martyrs rather than soldiers. Thus the Carthusians by vow abstain from flesh meat, so that they would rather die than eat meat, even where a physician judges it necessary for the preservation of health and life. For religious observance and the rule of life is more important than health and life.

And Mattathias learned. Here he, as though a Pontiff, interpreted the law of the sabbath for such a case—namely, that when one is attacked by an enemy it does not bind—and said it was better to fight for God and the law, lest all God's people be destroyed, and thus the Church and the worship of God perish. Wherefore the rest followed his ruling and determined to repel with arms those who attacked them on the sabbath day, as is clear from verse 41.

Then there was gathered to them the congregation of the Assidaeans, mighty men of Israel, every one that offered himself willingly for the law. — You ask who the Assidaeans are. Hugo, Lyranus, Dionysius, the Gloss, and St. Thomas in ch. 14 answer first that the Assidaeans are so called from assiduity, as though assiduous in the worship of God. But Assidaeans is a Hebrew word; wherefore it has a Hebrew etymology, not a Latin one.

Salianus, Delrio, and others hold that the Assideans are the Essenes, or kindred to the Essenes, and of the same institute and profession. But this is opposed first by the fact that the Essenes did not yet exist at that time, for they arose a little before Christ, as Baronius teaches. Second, because the Essenes were not strong in bodily strength, since abstinence, fasting, and vigils sapped their strength. Third, because the Essenes devoted themselves to God alone through prayer, reading, and contemplation. Hence Philo (in his book on the Essenes) says: 'Among them no one makes swords, spears, helmets, breastplates, shields, war-machines, or anything else useful for war.' The Assideans, however, abounded in these things, being devoted not to choir, as the Essenes were, but to war. I say therefore that the Assideans were soldiers of holy war, who gave their name to sacred military service for the sake of the law and the Synagogue — such as the knights of the Temple afterwards were, and as now are the military Orders of the Knights of Malta, of St. John, of St. Stephen, of the Teutonic Knights, and similar orders, who fight nobly for the Church of God against Turks, Saracens, and heretics. For when Antiochus was striving to overturn the law of God, the Temple, and the Synagogue, God raised up the Assideans to devote themselves to holy military service and to fight for the protection of the Temple and the law, offering themselves to all dangers of war. That this is so is shown: first, from the word 'synagogue,' meaning an assembly, a collection, a congregation of soldiers for the defense of the Synagogue; second, from the phrase 'strong in strength'; third, from the phrase 'volunteer for the law,' meaning one who voluntarily out of zeal would defend and champion the law. Wherefore the Assideans joined themselves to Judas Maccabaeus as to the leader of holy war, and were his chief soldiers, through whom he obtained so many and so great victories. This is clear from 2 Macc. 14:6: 'Those who are called Assideans, over whom Judas Maccabaeus presides, nourish wars and stir up seditions, and suffer not the kingdom to be in quiet.' These are the words of enemies who accused the Assideans as though seditious before King Demetrius.

[On the Assideans/Hasideans] They are called in Hebrew חֲסִידִים (chasidim). Some derive this from חֲסִידָה (chasida), meaning a stork or kite, because like kites they would swoop upon enemies and carry off plunder. Others interpret chasidim as "hunters" from צוּד (sud), meaning to hunt, because they hunted the enemies of Israel as a hunter pursues deer and hares. Better, Pagninus, Sanchez, Serarius and others interpret chasidim as pious, merciful, religious, holy, from חֶסֶד (chesed), meaning piety and mercy, because they waged a holy war on behalf of the Israelites. Hear now St. Chrysostom on Psalm 53: "When a heavy war bore down and the Maccabees could do nothing profitable, they hid themselves; for the Apostles did this too. For they did not always appear and rush into dangers openly, but sometimes withdrawing they fled and hid. But after they had rested a little, like noble whelps leaping from their dens and emerging from their lairs, they resolved no longer to save themselves alone, but also others as many as they could, and going about cities and the whole region they gathered as many as they found still sound and whole, and many also who were ailing and corrupted they restored to their former state, persuading them to return to the laws of their fathers."

[v. 1, "And they smote sinners"] That is, apostates who had defected from Judaism to Antiochus and to paganism. For this was the supreme sin and supreme iniquity, by which all religion, faith and virtue was betrayed and destroyed.

[v. 40, "With force"] Supply: they forcibly circumcised boys, as many as they found in the borders of Israel. That is, they circumcised boys by force, even against the will of their parents, because the parents were Israelites and therefore were obligated to circumcise their sons, and therefore could be compelled to do so by Mattathias as both leader and high priest; just as the Church now compels the faithful to present their sons for Baptism. But if there were among them sons of unbelievers, these too they circumcised as now their own, captured by right of war and separated from their parents. And although Lyra says this was done only to the sons of Jews, yet Scripture speaks absolutely; and although Dominic Soto in Book II on Baptism says: "If Mattathias did this to the sons of gentiles, he did it indeed according to zeal but not according to knowledge," yet this seems not rightly said of so distinguished and most holy a man. It is better therefore to assert that since they had already been reduced to servitude by right of war and separated from their parents, they could rightly be circumcised—just as we also now rightly baptize the sons of Saracens captured in war, as Suarez says, 3rd part, vol. 5, q. 67, art. 10, disp. 25, sect. 4.

[v. 48, "They obtained the law from the hand of the gentiles"] That is, the law of God and its worship, taken away by Antiochus and the gentiles, they restored to Israel by arms, and caused the law of God and its worship to recover its former dignity and veneration.

["And they gave not the horn to the sinner"] That is, they did not permit the horn—meaning the pride, strength and power of Antiochus—to grow; but these things, if they did not amputate them, they certainly cut back and greatly diminished, so that he could not and dared not rage and ravage in his accustomed manner. Learn here that horns must be cut from the proud, just as from horn-thrusting bulls: for whoever does not remove them from the proud and fierce, adds to and increases them; therefore whoever can, ought to remove them, lest those who grow fierce with them strike down and prostrate many.

[v. 69, "Chastisement...and the wrath of indignation"] Namely of God, with which God, angered and indignant at our sins, chastises us so harshly through Antiochus, giving him free rein to rage and be fierce against us. Thus Attila, king of the Huns, ravaging Gaul and Italy, when asked by St. Lupus, Bishop of Troyes, who he was and what he wanted, replied that he was the scourge of God to punish the crimes of the faithful. Thus Tamerlane was the scourge of God, and now the Ottomans are. Thus Nebuchadnezzar [was the scourge of God].
Alexander the Great, son of Philip of Macedon, coming from the land of Kittim, struck down Darius king of the Persians and Medes.

And give your lives [that is, your very existence] for the testament — that is, for the covenant, namely for the law of God: for the law was the condition of the covenant entered into by Abraham, Moses and the people with God.

And you shall receive great glory — not only in this world among men, for that glory is vain, brief and meagre, but also in heaven before God, the Angels and the saints: immense, celestial and eternal.

Was not Abraham found faithful in temptation — in which God tested his faith and obedience by commanding him to sacrifice to Him his only-begotten son Isaac, through whom He had promised him a numerous posterity, and indeed Christ Himself — "and it was reputed to him unto justice": that is to say, this act of Abraham's obedience and offering of Isaac was reckoned and esteemed by God to be an act of great justice, that is, of great merit.
Abraham was already just, yet through the test [of faith] he received a great increase of grace and justice, and became much more just and holy before God. See Romans 4:3 and Genesis 15:6.

Joseph in the time of his anguish (which he suffered in prison) kept the fifth commandment of the Decalogue: "Thou shalt not commit adultery," because he refused to lie with his master's wife when she solicited him; and therefore he was made lord of Egypt. Most excellently St. Cyprian in his tract On Mortality says: "Adversities are not scandals for you, but battles; let them not weaken or break the faith of a Christian, but rather let them display virtue in the struggle, since every injury of present evils must be despised in confidence of future goods. Unless the fight has gone before, there can be no victory; when victory has been achieved in the clash of battle, then the crown is given to the victors. The helmsman is known in the storm, the soldier is proved in the battle line." And further: "Adversities do not call us away from the truth of virtue and faith, but strengthen us in suffering. How great a sublimity to stand erect amid the ruins of the human race!" etc.

Phinehas our father (it is now evident that Mattathias and the Maccabees were descended from the tribe of Levi and from Phinehas the High Priest, and therefore when others fell away, the High Priesthood rightly belonged to them by succession) by being zealous with the zeal of God, killing the prince who was sacrificing to Baal-peor, received the testament, that is, God's promise and covenant of an eternal priesthood — namely that the high priesthood would endure in his descendants until the end of the Synagogue and Judaism, that is, until Christ. See the commentary on Numbers 25.

Jesus (Joshua, successor of Moses), when he fulfilled the word, that is, the commandment by which God through Moses ordered him to survey Canaan (Numbers 14), to defeat Amalek (Exodus 17), and after Moses to take charge of the people (Joshua 1), was made leader in Israel, succeeding Moses in the leadership of the people.

Caleb, when he testified in the assembly against his fellow spies who said that Canaan was unconquerable on account of its giant inhabitants and fortified cities, asserting that with God's help it could and would truly be conquered by the Hebrews as God had promised, received an inheritance peculiar to himself at Hebron (Numbers and Joshua 14:12). See the praises of Joshua and Caleb in Sirach 46.

David through his mercy — that is, by merit of his mercifulness, by which he was kind and merciful both toward others and toward Saul who persecuted him, refusing to harm him when he had him in his power — obtained the throne of the kingdom forever, so that he held royal power and transmitted it to his posterity, destined to endure for them as long as the kingdom of Israel and Judah lasted, namely until the Babylonian captivity. For "forever" here signifies not absolute perpetuity but relative and limited duration, namely perpetual duration throughout the whole state and kingdom of Judah, so that as long as it lasted, so long would the kingdom of David endure in it.

Elijah, while he was zealous with the zeal of the law, killing all the prophets and priests of Baal and opposing Ahab, Jezebel, and the Baalites (3 Kings 18), was received into heaven, caught up by a fiery chariot (4 Kings 2).
Ananias, Azarias, and Misael, believing in the true God and offering themselves to the flames for this faith, trusting that God could deliver them from the fire, were saved from the flame (Daniel 3).
Daniel in his simplicity — that is, in his integrity, candor, and constancy in persisting in worshipping only the true God — was for this merit delivered from the mouth of the lions (Daniel 6).

And from the words of the sinful man (the impious Antiochus) be not afraid, for his glory is dung and worms — that it should corrupt, putrefy, and stink, when shortly afterward through God's vengeance all his glory was turned to ignominy and all his fame to infamy, as is clear from what happened to Antiochus in Book 2, chapter 9.

You therefore, my sons, be strengthened and act manfully in the law (defending and fighting for it), for in it you shall be glorious — both by fighting and conquering, and by dying gloriously for it, as all of them died for it, namely Judas, Jonathan, and Simon. For what is more glorious than to fight and die for the law, for the fatherland, for God? Mattathias encourages his sons to the fierce struggles that threatened them with Antiochus, and therefore sets before them the examples of ancient heroes so that they might imitate them and attain similar glory; for nothing so animates one in difficult matters as the examples of ancestors. Hence Ovid in Tristia 4, elegy 5: "Fill the sad material with your virtues; glory travels by a steep and precipitous path. Who would have known Hector, if Troy had been fortunate? The public road of virtue is made through evils. Your art, Tiphys, lies idle if there be no waves on the sea; if men be healthy, your art, Phoebus, lies idle. Virtue, which lies hidden and rests inactive in good times and is not known, appears and is proved in evil times. Make use of the times whose gift you now enjoy, and a wide arena lies open for your praises."

And behold Simon your brother, I know that he is a man of counsel; hear him always, and he shall be a father to you — to guide you with his counsels as a father guides his sons, and likewise to govern my household and family as a father. Hence Josephus son of Gorion and Annius of Viterbo think Simon was the firstborn of Mattathias. But Josephus, Seder Olam Minor, and the Hebrews report that Judas was the firstborn, whence he also succeeded his father in both leadership and high priesthood; the second-born was John; the third was Jonathan; and the fourth was Simon.

And let Judas Maccabeus, mighty in strength from his youth, be your captain of the army. Hence it is evident that Judas performed illustrious deeds in battle not only in his father's old age, when he raised the standard against Antiochus shortly before his father's death, but also from his youth, relying on God's help; and therefore he inscribed on his banners "Maccabi," that is, mi camocha baelim Jehovah, meaning "Who is like unto thee among the mighty, O Lord?" (Exodus 15:11), whence he received the surname Maccabeus. For thus his dying father calls him here, and therefore appoints him commander of the war. See the commentary in the Proem.

And he died in the year 146. Namely in the same year in which he opposed Antiochus and took up the leadership together with the high priesthood. Some think he died from a wound received in battle; for since he had shortly before slain the Jew and Antiochus's prefect, and had roused all the Jews by going about among them and gathered them into a fortress against Antiochus, and performed many similar deeds requiring great bodily as well as spiritual strength, he does not seem to have been able so quickly to fail in strength from old age alone and to die, but rather to have received wounds from continual battles and died from them, or certainly to have been exhausted and to have died from unceasing labors and excessive fatigue. All agree that Mattathias was a priest, but Josephus, Eusebius, and Sigonius deny that he was high priest; however, the Hebrews and the Chaldean paraphrase on Canticles 6:6 assert that he was high priest, and Serarius proves this with many arguments. Indeed Judas succeeded his father in the high priesthood as well as in the military leadership, then Jonathan, and finally Simon, and Simon's sons after him down to Herod and Christ. Furthermore, the illustrious hero Mattathias died in the year 146 of the Greeks.

Machabaeus] Gorionides asserts that this name was first given here to Judas by his father Mattathias, and that it means "extinguisher," as if to say: You, O Judas, are called Machabaeus, that is, the extinguisher of Antiochus and of the other enemies of Israel. Others more correctly think that it had already been given to Judas before, derived from Machabi, which Judas inscribed on his standards; wherefore it was not bestowed upon him by Mattathias but rather confirmed as already given, and the very words sufficiently suggest this. See what was said in the Prooemium.

Et induit se lorica sicut gigas] First, because Judas was of tall and powerful body, and therefore in that age, when men fought not with cannons but with arms and swords, he was most valiant; whence he adds by way of explanation: Et protegebat castra gladio suo. Second, because he was of exceptional and giant-like strength, both of body and of soul; for God continually and unceasingly bestowed this upon him, since he trusted in God alone, for the protection of Israel. Thus Saul, David, Julius Caesar, Constantine, Heraclius, Charlemagne, and Godfrey of Bouillon were of tall and powerful body and spirit, and therefore outstanding warriors, slaying many enemies with their own hands and swords. For a giant is a symbol of rare strength and fortitude; giants of old were of the vastest size and most robust constitution, and therefore terrible to all, so that none dared oppose them. Such was Judas.

In the Old Law therefore priests and High Priests were often soldiers and military commanders, and Judas and his brothers were priests; they were chosen by God and the people precisely for this purpose, that they might raise up and support Israel, now almost prostrated and deserted by all. They were therefore primarily chosen as military commanders; the High Priesthood was added to them so that they might fight more ardently for religion, the Temple, and God against Antiochus, and for the other reasons enumerated above. Nor is this surprising; for the priesthood was then rude and carnal: priests were like butchers in slaughtering and flaying the sheep and oxen offered to God, and from this they learned to slaughter the enemies of Israel. For no soldiers are more fierce than butchers, who are constantly occupied with slaughter and blood. It is otherwise in the New Testament, where the priesthood is far more exalted and holy, and priests are occupied with consecrating and offering the Body of Christ in the Eucharist; wherefore it befits them to be, after the manner of Christ, meek, pious, and intent upon God, and therefore far removed from fighting and killing.

Similis factus est leoni] According to that prophecy of Judah son of Jacob the Patriarch, Genesis 49:9: A lion's cub is Judah; to the prey, my son, you have gone up; resting you crouched like a lion and like a lioness, who shall rouse him? Because in the place of this Judah, to whom Jacob attributed the principate, our Judas Machabaeus succeeded as leader and prince of the people, though not sprung from Judah but from Levi, and in strength, might, and victories he was not inferior but superior. The Hebrews relate that Judas bore a lion as his emblem on his standards.

Et sicut catulus leonis rugiens in venatione] In Greek: εἰς θήραν, that is, into the hunt or into the prey. For lion cubs, when their stomachs growl with hunger, roar, and roaring rush upon sheep and cattle to satisfy their hunger.

Et directa est salus in manu ejus] As if to say: Rightly and directly did salvation come to Israel through the hand, might, and battles of Judas; for Judas by his industry and fortitude rightly directed Israel toward salvation, and caused them to tend toward it by a straight path and course. It may secondly be explained by hypallage thus: the hand of Judas was directed toward bringing salvation to Israel. For God directed the hand of Judas to continuous victories, by which salvation came directly to Israel. In Greek for directa is εὐόδωσεν, that is, proceeded by an easy road, was made prosperous; whence εὐοδία is a prosperous journey, likewise prosperity. Again, salvation here may be taken for victory, by metonymy; for in this consisted the salvation and security of the people. Thus salvation is taken for victory by metonymy: Judges 15:18; 1 Kings 11:9; 14:45; 17:47; and 1 Maccabees 4:25.

Exacerbavit reges multos] That is, he irritated, harassed, and provoked to wrath and indignation many kings, namely Antiochus Epiphanes and his son Antiochus Eupator, and Demetrius, and their neighbors and allies; for against these he engaged in many battles, always conquering and triumphing.
Virtutem multam] In Greek δύναμιν, that is, a great force of soldiers.

Et cognovit Judas, et exiit obviam illi, et percussit et occidit illum] Apollonius the commander of the enemy. Hear Josephus Gorionides narrating the matter vividly: When Judas saw Apollonius standing in the midst of his army, he charged at him with the greatest fury all the way to the middle of the camp, penetrating everything, striking down the most powerful of the enemy to right and to left, just as a reaper lays low the sheaves and ears of his harvest, and advancing to Apollonius he struck him and laid him low with his sword.

Et gladium Apollonii abstulit Judas, et erat pugnans in eo omnibus diebus] First, because this sword, belonging to so great a commander, was remarkable, large, strong, and sharp; second, as a sign of victory and trophy; third, so that at the sight of it Judas might rouse himself and his men to fight bravely and conquer in subsequent battles, just as they had conquered and slain Apollonius and his men when they wrested this sword from him.

Et audivit Seron] This is the second commander of Antiochus, whom with great forces he attacked; Judas with a few men, and those exhausted by fasting as is said in verse 17, struck him powerfully and slew him, because, says Gorionides, they had marched all night and had arrived fasting.

Facile est concludi multos in manu paucorum, et non est differentia in conspectu Dei coeli liberare in multis et in paucis] Because God can as easily apply his power, aid, and victory to few as to many; for one soldier furnished with God's strength can as easily lay low twenty or a hundred as two hundred lacking that same strength of God can do so. Therefore God, who chooses the weak things of the world to confound the strong, often selects few men and through them lays low many, to show that the few accomplish this not by their own strength but by God's, and so that the few who are about to fight against many may invoke God and hope for victory from him. Hence God commanded Gideon to advance with three hundred unarmed men against one hundred and twenty thousand Midianites, all of whom Gideon slew through the three hundred, Judges 7. Indeed even excellent military commanders select few soldiers, but veterans, robust, and ready either to conquer or die, for great undertakings; for among many there are many who are timid, prone to flight, and disorderly, who confuse and disturb the battle line, so that they are easily defeated by a few valiant and well-ordered men. Alexander the Great with forty thousand veterans overthrew Darius equipped with many hundreds of thousands, and indeed subdued virtually the whole world. Leonidas with three hundred slew the countless forces of Xerxes at Thermopylae. Abraham with three hundred of his servants defeated four kings, Genesis 14. # GEM

De coelo fortitudo est] Angels were accustomed to fight for the people of God. Hence in 2 Maccabees 10:29 and 38 we read that five men from heaven on horses adorned with golden bridles gave leadership to the Jews.
